and also known as **Gold Coast 2018**, were an international [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") for members of the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") that was held on the [Gold Coast, Queensland](/wiki/Gold_Coast,_Queensland "Gold Coast, Queensland"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), between 4 and 15 April 2018. It was the fifth time Australia had hosted the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games") and the first time a major multi-sport had an equal number of events for male and female athletes.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

The **2018 Commonwealth Games**, officially known as the **XXI Commonwealth Games** and also known as **Gold Coast 2018**, were an international [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") for members of the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") that was held on the [Gold Coast, Queensland](/wiki/Gold_Coast,_Queensland "Gold Coast, Queensland"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), between 4 and 15 April 2018. It was the fifth time Australia had hosted the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games") and the first time a major multi-sport had an equal number of events for male and female athletes.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

4,426 athletes including 300 para-athletes from 71 Commonwealth Games Associations took part in the event.[[2]](#cite_note-2) [The Gambia](/wiki/The_Gambia "The Gambia"), which withdrew its membership from the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") and [Commonwealth Games Federation](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Federation "Commonwealth Games Federation") (CGF) in 2013, was readmitted on 31 March 2018 and participated in the event.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) With 275 sets of medals, the games featured 18 [Commonwealth sports](/wiki/Commonwealth_Sports "Commonwealth Sports"), including [beach volleyball](/wiki/Beach_volleyball "Beach volleyball"), [para triathlon](/wiki/Paratriathlon "Paratriathlon") and women's [rugby sevens](/wiki/Rugby_sevens "Rugby sevens"). These sporting events took place at 14 venues in the host city, two venues in [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane") and one venue each in [Cairns](/wiki/Cairns "Cairns") and [Townsville](/wiki/Townsville "Townsville").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

This was the first Commonwealth Games to take place under the CGF presidency of [Dame](/wiki/Dame "Dame") [Louise Martin](/wiki/Louise_Martin_(sports_administrator) "Louise Martin (sports administrator)").[[5]](#cite_note-5) The host city Gold Coast was announced at the CGF General Assembly in [Basseterre](/wiki/Basseterre "Basseterre"), [Saint Kitts](/wiki/Saint_Kitts "Saint Kitts"), on 11 November 2011.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Gold Coast became the seventh Oceanian city and the first regional city to host the Commonwealth Games. These were the eighth Games to be held in [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania") and the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere").

The host nation [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") topped the medal table for the fourth time in the past five Commonwealth Games, winning the most golds (80) and most medals overall (198). [England](/wiki/England_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games "England at the 2018 Commonwealth Games") and [India](/wiki/India_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games "India at the 2018 Commonwealth Games") finished second and third respectively.[[7]](#cite_note-7) [Vanuatu](/wiki/Vanuatu_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games "Vanuatu at the 2018 Commonwealth Games"), [Cook Islands](/wiki/Cook_Islands_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games "Cook Islands at the 2018 Commonwealth Games"), [Solomon Islands](/wiki/Solomon_Islands_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games "Solomon Islands at the 2018 Commonwealth Games"), [British Virgin Islands](/wiki/British_Virgin_Islands_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games "British Virgin Islands at the 2018 Commonwealth Games") and [Dominica](/wiki/Dominica_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games "Dominica at the 2018 Commonwealth Games") each won their first Commonwealth Games medals.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The 2018 Commonwealth Games boosted the Queensland economy by more than [A$](/wiki/Australian_dollar "Australian dollar")2.4 billion between 2013 and 2022,[[9]](#cite_note-9) while the venues constructed and upgraded for the Games generated over $60 million in economic benefit annually to the Gold Coast, with the success of the 2018 Commonwealth Games credited with helping Brisbane to secure hosting rights for the [2032 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2032_Summer_Olympics "2032 Summer Olympics").[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Host selection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Host selection")]

Main article: [Bids for the 2018 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Bids_for_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games "Bids for the 2018 Commonwealth Games")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Gold_Coast_skyline_%28Unsplash%29.jpg/250px-Gold_Coast_skyline_%28Unsplash%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gold_Coast_skyline_(Unsplash).jpg)

Gold Coast was selected by the [Australian Commonwealth Games Association](/wiki/Australian_Commonwealth_Games_Association "Australian Commonwealth Games Association") as the official bid city from Australia for the 2018 Commonwealth Games

On 22 August 2008, the [Premier of Queensland](/wiki/Premier_of_Queensland "Premier of Queensland"), [Anna Bligh](/wiki/Anna_Bligh "Anna Bligh"), officially launched Gold Coast City's bid to host the Commonwealth Games in 2018. On 7 April 2009, the [ABC](/wiki/Australian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Australian Broadcasting Corporation") reported a [land exchange](/wiki/Land_exchange "Land exchange") deal between Gold Coast City and [State of Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland") for [Carrara Stadium](/wiki/Carrara_Stadium "Carrara Stadium"). According to Mayor [Ron Clarke](/wiki/Ron_Clarke "Ron Clarke"), the land would aid a potential bid for the 2018 Commonwealth Games.

The land exchanged would be used as the site of an aquatics centre. In the same article, Mayor Clarke raised the question of the Australian Federal Government's commitment to a Commonwealth Games bid in light of the Government's support for [Australia's 2018 FIFA World Cup Finals bid](/wiki/Australian_2018_and_2022_FIFA_World_Cup_bid "Australian 2018 and 2022 FIFA World Cup bid").[[12]](#cite_note-abc.net.au-12) On 16 April 2009, Queensland Premier Anna Bligh told reporters that a successful Commonwealth Games bid by Gold Coast City could help the tourist strip win a role in hosting the World Cup.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Clarke said "some of the infrastructure that would be built for the Commonwealth Games will be useful for Gold Coast City to get a World Cup game out of the soccer World Cup if we're successful as a nation." However, the decision on the venues for the 2018 and 2022 FIFA World Cups were made eleven months prior to the bid decision for the 2018 Commonwealth Games, so the potential World Cup venues had already been chosen. On 3 June 2009, Gold Coast City was confirmed as Australia's exclusive bidder vying for the 2018 Commonwealth Games.[[14]](#cite_note-14) "Should a bid proceed, Gold Coast City will have the exclusive Australian rights to bid as host city for 2018," Bligh stated. "Recently I met with the president and CEO of the Australian Commonwealth Games Association and we agreed to commission a full and comprehensive feasibility study into the potential for the 2018 Commonwealth Games," she said. "Under the stewardship of Queensland Events new chair, [Geoff Dixon](/wiki/Geoff_Dixon "Geoff Dixon"), that study is now well advanced." On 15 March 2010, it was announced that the Queensland Government will provide initial funding of A$11 million for the 2018 Commonwealth Games bid.

The Premier of Queensland has indicated the Government's support for the bid to the Australian Commonwealth Games Association.[[15]](#cite_note-goldcoast.com.au-15) On 31 March 2010, the Australian Commonwealth Games Association officially launched the bid to host the 2018 Commonwealth Games.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In October 2011, Gold Coast City Mayor [Ron Clarke](/wiki/Ron_Clarke "Ron Clarke") stated that the games would provide a strong legacy for the city after the games have ended.[[17]](#cite_note-Legacy_benefits_to_Gold_Coast_of_2018_Commonwealth_Games_will_be_huge,_says_city's_Mayor-17)

On 31 March 2010, a surprise bid was made for the 2018 Commonwealth Games by [Hambantota](/wiki/Hambantota "Hambantota") in [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"). Hambantota was devastated by the [2004 Indian Ocean Tsunami](/wiki/2004_Indian_Ocean_Tsunami "2004 Indian Ocean Tsunami"), and was undergoing a major face lift. The first phase of the [Hambantota International Port](/wiki/Hambantota_International_Port "Hambantota International Port") was nearing completion at the time, and a new [cricket stadium](/wiki/Hambantota_International_Cricket_Stadium "Hambantota International Cricket Stadium") had also been built, which had hosted matches in the [2011 Cricket World Cup](/wiki/2011_Cricket_World_Cup "2011 Cricket World Cup").

On 10 November 2011, the Hambantota bidders claimed they had already secured enough votes to win the hosting rights.[[18]](#cite_note-Sri_Lanka_boasting_of_Games_bid_win-18) However, on 11 November it was officially announced [Gold Coast City](/wiki/Gold_Coast,_Queensland "Gold Coast, Queensland") had won the rights to host the games.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-Coast_wins_2018_Commonwealth_Games-20)

## Development and preparation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Development and preparation")]

The event was overseen by the Gold Coast 2018 Commonwealth Games Corporation (GOLDOC). The GOLDOC was formed in 2012 by the [Government of Queensland](/wiki/Government_of_Queensland "Government of Queensland").[[21]](#cite_note-21) Its headquarters were located in [Ashmore](/wiki/Ashmore,_Queensland "Ashmore, Queensland"), a suburban region of Gold Coast.[[22]](#cite_note-22) In February 2012, [Mark Peters](/wiki/Mark_Peters_(sport_administrator) "Mark Peters (sport administrator)") was appointed chief executive officer of the GOLDOC.[[23]](#cite_note-23) The Queensland Government Minister [Kate Jones](/wiki/Kate_Jones_(politician) "Kate Jones (politician)") was tasked with overseeing the Games.[[24]](#cite_note-24) [Peter Beattie](/wiki/Peter_Beattie "Peter Beattie") [AC](/wiki/Companion_of_the_Order_of_Australia "Companion of the Order of Australia") served as the chairman of GOLDOC who was appointed on 17 May 2016 to replace Nigel Chamier [OAM](/wiki/Order_of_Australia_Medal "Order of Australia Medal").[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

#### Venues on the Gold Coast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Venues on the Gold Coast")]

[Carrara Stadium](/wiki/Carrara_Stadium "Carrara Stadium"), located in the suburb of [Carrara](/wiki/Carrara,_Queensland "Carrara, Queensland"), was the main venue for [athletics](/wiki/Athletics_(sport) "Athletics (sport)"), the [opening ceremony](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games_opening_ceremony "2018 Commonwealth Games opening ceremony") and the [closing ceremony](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games_closing_ceremony "2018 Commonwealth Games closing ceremony"). The seating capacity of the stadium was temporarily increased to 40,000 for the Games by the installation of a large temporary stand in the north end.[[30]](#cite_note-30) The new [Carrara Sports and Leisure Centre](/wiki/Carrara_Sports_and_Leisure_Centre "Carrara Sports and Leisure Centre") hosted the [Badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton"), [Para Powerlifting](/wiki/Paralympic_powerlifting "Paralympic powerlifting"), [Weightlifting](/wiki/Olympic_weightlifting "Olympic weightlifting") and [Wrestling](/wiki/Wrestling "Wrestling") events.[[31]](#cite_note-31) The [Carrara Indoor Sports Stadium](/wiki/Carrara_Indoor_Stadium "Carrara Indoor Stadium") hosted the back-of-house event operations.

The [Gold Coast Convention and Exhibition Centre](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Convention_and_Exhibition_Centre "Gold Coast Convention and Exhibition Centre"), located in the suburb of [Broadbeach](/wiki/Broadbeach,_Queensland "Broadbeach, Queensland"), hosted the [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") finals and [netball](/wiki/Netball "Netball") preliminaries and also served as the Main Media Centre and International Broadcast centre hosting over 3,000 members of the worlds press.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The [Broadbeach Bowls Club](/wiki/Broadbeach_Bowls_Club "Broadbeach Bowls Club") hosted the [Bowls](/wiki/Bowls "Bowls") competition.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

The [Nerang National Park](/wiki/Nerang_National_Park "Nerang National Park") was the location for the mountain bike competition. A new course was constructed to meet international competition requirements and temporary spectator seating for 2,000 spectators.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

The newly built [Coomera Indoor Sports Centre](/wiki/Coomera_Indoor_Sports_Centre "Coomera Indoor Sports Centre") hosted [gymnastics](/wiki/Gymnastics "Gymnastics") and netball finals.[[35]](#cite_note-35) The [Oxenford Studios](/wiki/Oxenford_Studios "Oxenford Studios") hosted the sports of [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), [table tennis](/wiki/Table_tennis "Table tennis") and [squash](/wiki/Squash_(sport) "Squash (sport)").[[36]](#cite_note-36) During Games mode, the venue was enhanced to provide for each sporting federation's technical venue requirements and provide spectator seating. The [Gold Coast Hockey Centre](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Hockey_Centre "Gold Coast Hockey Centre") hosted the men's and women's [field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey") events.[[37]](#cite_note-37) The [Southport Broadwater Parklands](/wiki/Southport_Broadwater_Parklands "Southport Broadwater Parklands") hosted [Triathlon](/wiki/Triathlon_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games "Triathlon at the 2018 Commonwealth Games"), [Marathon](/wiki/Marathon "Marathon") and [Racewalking](/wiki/Racewalking "Racewalking") events.[[38]](#cite_note-38) The [Optus aquatic centre](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Aquatic_Centre "Gold Coast Aquatic Centre") hosted the [Swimming](/wiki/Swimming_(sport) "Swimming (sport)") and [Diving](/wiki/Diving_(sport) "Diving (sport)") events.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

[Robina Stadium](/wiki/Robina_Stadium "Robina Stadium") hosted the [rugby sevens](/wiki/Rugby_sevens "Rugby sevens") competition, and was upgraded to meet [World Rugby](/wiki/World_Rugby "World Rugby") standards.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The [Currumbin](/wiki/Currumbin,_Queensland "Currumbin, Queensland") Beachfront hosted the road racing elements of the cycling programme.[[41]](#cite_note-41) [Coolangatta](/wiki/Coolangatta "Coolangatta") Beachfront hosted the [Beach volleyball](/wiki/Beach_volleyball "Beach volleyball") event.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

#### Venues outside Gold Coast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Venues outside Gold Coast")]

[Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane"), along with the Gold Coast, forms part of the [South East Queensland](/wiki/South_East_Queensland "South East Queensland") [conurbation](/wiki/Conurbation "Conurbation"). [Track cycling](/wiki/Track_cycling "Track cycling") was held at the [Sleeman Sports Complex](/wiki/Sleeman_Centre_(Brisbane) "Sleeman Centre (Brisbane)") in the suburb of [Chandler](/wiki/Chandler,_Queensland "Chandler, Queensland"), where a new [velodrome](/wiki/Sleeman_Sports_Complex#Anna_Meares_Velodrome "Sleeman Sports Complex") was built.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

The [shooting](/wiki/Shooting_sports "Shooting sports") disciplines were held at the [Belmont Shooting Centre](/wiki/Belmont,_Queensland "Belmont, Queensland"). In [Tropical North Queensland](/wiki/Tropical_North_Queensland "Tropical North Queensland"), the [Cairns Convention Centre](/wiki/Cairns_Convention_Centre "Cairns Convention Centre") and [Townsville Entertainment Centre](/wiki/Townsville_Entertainment_Centre "Townsville Entertainment Centre") hosted the preliminary rounds of [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball").[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)

### Athletes village

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Athletes village")]

Main article: [2018 Commonwealth Games Athletes Village](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games_Athletes_Village "2018 Commonwealth Games Athletes Village")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/The_2018_Commonwealth_Games_Village%2C_Parklands%2C_Southport%2C_Gold_Coast%2C_Queensland_05.jpg/250px-The_2018_Commonwealth_Games_Village%2C_Parklands%2C_Southport%2C_Gold_Coast%2C_Queensland_05.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_2018_Commonwealth_Games_Village,_Parklands,_Southport,_Gold_Coast,_Queensland_05.jpg)

The [2018 Commonwealth Games Athletes Village](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games_Athletes_Village "2018 Commonwealth Games Athletes Village")

The Athletes Village was officially opened from 25 March 2018 and provided accommodation and services to 6,600 athletes and officials in 1252 permanent dwellings. It was located in [Southport](/wiki/Southport,_Queensland "Southport, Queensland"), [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast,_Queensland "Gold Coast, Queensland").[[47]](#cite_note-:102-47) There were 1,170 one and two-bedroom apartments and 82 three-bedroom townhouses. The village had three zones - International, Residential and Operational.

The residential zone accommodated athletes and officials and also offered recreation, gym and medical facilities. The equipment in the gym was provided by [Technogym](/wiki/Technogym "Technogym").[[48]](#cite_note-48) Adjoining the gym was the Athlete Recovery Area. The International Zone consisted of retail services, shops and the main dining hall. The Festival 2018 events were also held in the International Zone. The Dining hall served over 18,000 meals per day to the athletes during the Games.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Australian telecommunications company [Optus](/wiki/Optus "Optus") opened a store in the International zone named "Yes Optus Store" which provided free calling services to the athletes and officials and other services such as phone charging and watching events of the Games in televisions.[[50]](#cite_note-50) Optus also provided free Wi-Fi services in the village.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

### Financing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Financing")]

The sports-related costs of Gold Coast 2018 was [A$](/wiki/Australian_dollar "Australian dollar")1.5 billion ([US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")1.2 billion).[[52]](#cite_note-:9-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)

### Countdown

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Countdown")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Streets_Beach_%2838336072865%29.jpg/250px-Streets_Beach_%2838336072865%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Streets_Beach_(38336072865).jpg)

Countdown clock in [Streets Beach at South Bank Parklands](/wiki/South_Bank_Parklands "South Bank Parklands")

The countdown clocks were unveiled on 4 April 2013, exactly five years from the opening ceremony of the Games. The clocks were shaped as a surfboard and were located at the beach end of [Cavill Avenue](/wiki/Cavill_Avenue "Cavill Avenue") in [Surfers Paradise](/wiki/Surfers_Paradise,_Queensland "Surfers Paradise, Queensland") and in [South Bank Parklands](/wiki/South_Bank_Parklands "South Bank Parklands") in [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane"). The Countdown Clock was the first fixed element of the Commonwealth Games visual identity program.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

### Ticketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Ticketing")]

The ticket requests began on 24 April 2017 and ended on 22 May 2017.[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) The first round of tickets were allocated on 22 June 2017 via a computer-generated ballot system. About 70% of the people who applied for the tickets had received some or all of the tickets requested in the first phase.[[57]](#cite_note-57) In Australia, ticket prices ranged from A$10 for many events to A$495 for the most expensive seats at the opening ceremony.[[58]](#cite_note-58) The games expected to have 1.2 million tickets for sale. Around 1.06 million tickets were sold by 3 April 2018.[[59]](#cite_note-59) [Ticketek](/wiki/Ticketek "Ticketek") was the provider of ticketing services for the Games.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

### Queen's baton relay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Queen's baton relay")]

Main article: [2018 Commonwealth Games Queen's baton relay](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games_Queen%27s_baton_relay "2018 Commonwealth Games Queen's baton relay")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Glenn_McGrath%2C_Peter_Beattie_and_Leisel_Jones_at_the_Queen%27s_Baton_Relay%2C_Sydney%2C_Australia%2C_2018-02-03.jpg/250px-Glenn_McGrath%2C_Peter_Beattie_and_Leisel_Jones_at_the_Queen%27s_Baton_Relay%2C_Sydney%2C_Australia%2C_2018-02-03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Glenn_McGrath,_Peter_Beattie_and_Leisel_Jones_at_the_Queen%27s_Baton_Relay,_Sydney,_Australia,_2018-02-03.jpg)

[Glenn McGrath](/wiki/Glenn_McGrath "Glenn McGrath") holding the Queen's baton with [Peter Beattie](/wiki/Peter_Beattie "Peter Beattie") (GOLDOC chairman) and [Leisel Jones](/wiki/Leisel_Jones "Leisel Jones") in [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/The_2018_Queen%27s_Baton_Relay_passing_in_front_of_Helensvale_Library.jpg/250px-The_2018_Queen%27s_Baton_Relay_passing_in_front_of_Helensvale_Library.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_2018_Queen%27s_Baton_Relay_passing_in_front_of_Helensvale_Library.jpg)

The 2018 Queen's Baton Relay passing in front of [Helensvale](/wiki/Helensvale,_Queensland "Helensvale, Queensland") Library

The Queen's baton of the 2018 Commonwealth Games had a distinctive loop design and was made of macadamia wood and recycled plastic sourced from Gold Coast waterways. Its design was inspired by the Queensland's "vibrant spirit and indigenous heritage" and with sustainability.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

The design of the baton was unveiled on 20 November 2016 at a ceremony at the [Jupiter Gold Coast hotel](/wiki/The_Star_Gold_Coast "The Star Gold Coast").[[62]](#cite_note-62) The baton was designed by the Brisbane-based firm Designworks.[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64) At the 2018 Good Design Awards conducted by the Good Design Australia, the baton won the Best Product Sport and Lifestyle award.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

The Queen's baton relay was launched on [Commonwealth Day](/wiki/Commonwealth_Day "Commonwealth Day"), 13 March 2017, on the historic forecourt at [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace") in [London](/wiki/London "London"), signalling the official countdown to the start of the Games. Accompanied by the [Duke of Edinburgh](/wiki/Prince_Philip,_Duke_of_Edinburgh "Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh") and [Prince Edward](/wiki/Prince_Edward,_Duke_of_Edinburgh "Prince Edward, Duke of Edinburgh"), Her Majesty [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_II "Queen Elizabeth II") heralded the start of the relay by placing her 'message to the Commonwealth and its athletes' into the distinctive loop-design Queen's Baton which then set off on its journey around the globe.[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67)

The baton traveled for 388 days, spending time in every nation and territory of the Commonwealth. The Gold Coast 2018 Queen's Baton Relay was the longest in Commonwealth Games history. Covering 230,000 km over 388 days, the baton made its way through the six Commonwealth regions of Africa, the Americas, the Caribbean, Europe, Asia and Oceania.[[68]](#cite_note-68) The baton landed on Australian soil in December 2017 and then spent 100 days travelling through Australia, finishing its journey at the [opening ceremony](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games_opening_ceremony "2018 Commonwealth Games opening ceremony") on 4 April 2018, where the message was removed from the Baton and read aloud by [Charles, Prince of Wales](/wiki/Charles,_Prince_of_Wales "Charles, Prince of Wales").[[69]](#cite_note-69)

[Tourism Australia](/wiki/Tourism_Australia "Tourism Australia") and [QSuper](/wiki/QSuper "QSuper") sponsored the international and Australian segment of Queen's Baton Relay respectively, while [Longines](/wiki/Longines "Longines") sponsored both the segments.[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72)

### Medals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Medals")]

The medals for the Games were officially unveiled at a charity gala held on 4 November 2017. Australian Indigenous artist Delvene Cockatoo-Collins designed the medals, while they were produced by the [Royal Australian Mint](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Mint "Royal Australian Mint"). The design of the medals was inspired by the coastline of [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast,_Australia "Gold Coast, Australia") along with Indigenous culture.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

Furthermore, Cockatoo-Collins mentioned, "the medal design represents soft sand lines which shift with every tide and wave, also symbolic of athletic achievement, The continual change of tide represents the evolution in athletes who are making their mark, Records are made and special moments of elation are celebrated". Approximately 1,500 medals were created to be distributed to the medalists and each measures approximately 63 millimetres in diameter. The medals weigh between 138 and 163 grams.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

### Volunteering

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Volunteering")]

Over 15,000 volunteers were hired for the Games. Over 45,000 applicants applied to become a volunteer. The uniforms for the volunteers were revealed on 11 November 2017 at the [Gold Coast Convention and Exhibition Centre](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Convention_and_Exhibition_Centre "Gold Coast Convention and Exhibition Centre"). Former [Gold Coast Titans](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Titans "Gold Coast Titans") player [Mat Rogers](/wiki/Mat_Rogers "Mat Rogers") who was present at the uniform showcase ceremony, said that the uniforms were "very Gold Coast" and it was like an "active wear".[[75]](#cite_note-75) [Hard Yakka](/wiki/Hard_Yakka "Hard Yakka") was the official supplier of the uniforms for the volunteers.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

### Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/FC_2_test%2C_surfers_paradise_boulevard%2C_March_2014.JPG/250px-FC_2_test%2C_surfers_paradise_boulevard%2C_March_2014.JPG)](/wiki/File:FC_2_test,_surfers_paradise_boulevard,_March_2014.JPG)

[Gold Coast light rail](/wiki/G:link "G:link")

During the Games period, free public transportation within [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland") region was provided to ticket and accreditation holders. The free transportation services were available on local buses, train and [Gold Coast light rail](/wiki/Gold_Coast_light_rail "Gold Coast light rail") (G:link) services on the Gold Coast and on [TransLink](/wiki/TransLink_(South_East_Queensland) "TransLink (South East Queensland)") and [Qconnect](/wiki/QConnect "QConnect") bus services in Cairns and Townsville.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

The Gold Coast light rail system, connected a number of the key Games venues, including the [Gold Coast Aquatic Centre](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Aquatic_Centre "Gold Coast Aquatic Centre"), [Broadwater Parklands](/wiki/Southport_Broadwater_Parklands "Southport Broadwater Parklands") and the [Gold Coast Convention & Exhibition Centre](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Convention_%26_Exhibition_Centre "Gold Coast Convention & Exhibition Centre"), with the major accommodation centres of [Surfers Paradise](/wiki/Surfers_Paradise "Surfers Paradise") and [Broadbeach](/wiki/Broadbeach,_Queensland "Broadbeach, Queensland") and the Athletes Village at Parklands. An extension to the system was announced in October 2015, connecting the then current terminus at [Gold Coast University Hospital](/wiki/Gold_Coast_University_Hospital "Gold Coast University Hospital") to the [railway line to Brisbane](/wiki/Gold_Coast_railway_line "Gold Coast railway line") at [Helensvale](/wiki/Helensvale_railway_station "Helensvale railway station"). The extension opened in December 2017, in time for the games.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

Four temporary Games lanes were introduced along the M1 and activated at different times to decrease traffic disruptions for road users. These were used by police and emergency vehicles, spectator shuttle buses and accredited Games family and athletes.[[79]](#cite_note-79) The [Gold Coast Airport](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Airport "Gold Coast Airport") served as the official airport for the Games.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

### Security

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Security")]

A total of 3,500 police officers from Queensland and 4,000 security personnel from the Australian security companies MSS Security, [Wilson Security](/wiki/Wilson_Security "Wilson Security"), SecureCorp and SNP were present at the Games.[[81]](#cite_note-81) The [Australian Defence Force](/wiki/Australian_Defence_Force "Australian Defence Force") (ADF) assisted Gold Coast authorities in ensuring the security of the Games and deployed over 1,000 personnel to help with the effort.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

Furthermore, Operation ATLAS was the codename for the ADF contribution to the security of the Games.[[83]](#cite_note-83) The [Australian Federal Police](/wiki/Australian_Federal_Police "Australian Federal Police") and [Australian Border Force](/wiki/Australian_Border_Force "Australian Border Force") assisted the [Queensland Police Service](/wiki/Queensland_Police_Service "Queensland Police Service") (QPS) who were the lead agency of the Games' security.[[84]](#cite_note-84) American cyber security software company [Symantec](/wiki/NortonLifeLock "NortonLifeLock") provided IT and cyber security services for the Games.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

### Anti-doping

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Anti-doping")]

The [Australian Sports Anti-Doping Authority](/wiki/Australian_Sports_Anti-Doping_Authority "Australian Sports Anti-Doping Authority") conducted an anti-doping drive in the months prior to the Games, covering around 2500 tests of Australian athletes, as well as 500 tests against international athletes. Three Australians failed drug tests in this process, along with around 20 international athletes, subject to appeal. The Commonwealth Games Federation conducted in-competition testing and, matching protocol at the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games"), launched a sample storage initiative to allow for future testing of samples up to ten years later, should detection technology improve.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

### Festival 2018

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Festival 2018")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Festival_2018_stage_at_the_Surfers_Paradise_beach%2C_Queensland_during_2018_Commonwealth_Games.jpg/250px-Festival_2018_stage_at_the_Surfers_Paradise_beach%2C_Queensland_during_2018_Commonwealth_Games.jpg)](/wiki/File:Festival_2018_stage_at_the_Surfers_Paradise_beach,_Queensland_during_2018_Commonwealth_Games.jpg)

Festival 2018 stage at the Surfers Paradise beach, Queensland during the Games

The Festival 2018 was a 12-day multi-arts program for the 2018 Commonwealth Games which included free musical performances, activities, public art and other family-friendly events.[[87]](#cite_note-87) The program took place at [Surfers Paradise](/wiki/Surfers_Paradise,_Queensland "Surfers Paradise, Queensland") and [Broadbeach](/wiki/Broadbeach,_Queensland "Broadbeach, Queensland") on the Gold Coast, Cultural Forecourt, [South Bank](/wiki/South_Bank,_Queensland "South Bank, Queensland") in Brisbane, Lagoon Precinct in Cairns and [Jezzine Barracks](/wiki/Jezzine_Barracks "Jezzine Barracks"), Strand Park and [Queens Gardens](/wiki/Queens_Gardens,_Townsville "Queens Gardens, Townsville") in Townsville.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

### Sustainability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Sustainability")]

The GOLDOC delivered the event with a focus on [sustainability](/wiki/Sustainability "Sustainability") under the guidance of the [ISO 20121](/wiki/ISO_20121 "ISO 20121") event sustainability management system and the [Global Reporting Initiative](/wiki/Global_Reporting_Initiative "Global Reporting Initiative") (GRI) framework Sustainability Reporting Standards.[[89]](#cite_note-89) The GOLDOC received the Sustainability Award in the Australian Business Awards 2016 for focusing on sustainable practices and planning in the preparation of the games.[[90]](#cite_note-90) The GOLDOC headquarters received the 4 Star Green Star – Interiors PILOT rating from the Green Building Council of Australia.[[91]](#cite_note-91) The new [Anna Meares Velodrome](/wiki/Anna_Meares_Velodrome "Anna Meares Velodrome"), built specifically for the games, is the first velodrome in the world to have full LED broadcast-quality lighting that cuts energy consumption by up to 60% and reduces running costs and carbon emissions.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

### Opening ceremony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Opening ceremony")]

Main articles: [2018 Commonwealth Games opening ceremony](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games_opening_ceremony "2018 Commonwealth Games opening ceremony") and [2018 Commonwealth Games Parade of Nations](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games_Parade_of_Nations "2018 Commonwealth Games Parade of Nations")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/2018_Commonwealth_Games_opening_ceremony.jpeg/250px-2018_Commonwealth_Games_opening_ceremony.jpeg)](/wiki/File:2018_Commonwealth_Games_opening_ceremony.jpeg)

[Opening ceremony](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games_opening_ceremony "2018 Commonwealth Games opening ceremony") of the 2018 Commonwealth Games at the [Carrara Stadium](/wiki/Carrara_Stadium "Carrara Stadium")

The opening ceremony was held at [Carrara Stadium](/wiki/Carrara_Stadium "Carrara Stadium") on the Gold Coast, between 20:00 and 22:40 AEST, on 4 April 2018.[[93]](#cite_note-93) The [Head of the Commonwealth](/wiki/Head_of_the_Commonwealth "Head of the Commonwealth"), Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II"), was represented by her son, [Charles, Prince of Wales](/wiki/Charles,_Prince_of_Wales "Charles, Prince of Wales").[[94]](#cite_note-94) [David Zolkwer](/wiki/David_Zolkwer "David Zolkwer") was its artistic director,[[95]](#cite_note-95) with music direction by [Katie Noonan](/wiki/Katie_Noonan "Katie Noonan").[[96]](#cite_note-96) Live musical performers included [Christine Anu](/wiki/Christine_Anu "Christine Anu"), [Delta Goodrem](/wiki/Delta_Goodrem "Delta Goodrem"), [Katie Noonan](/wiki/Katie_Noonan "Katie Noonan"), [Ricki-Lee Coulter](/wiki/Ricki-Lee_Coulter "Ricki-Lee Coulter") and [Ruel](/wiki/Ruel_(singer) "Ruel (singer)") who performed "Golden Years" as the closing act.[[97]](#cite_note-97) The ceremony transmitted live on [Channel 7](/wiki/Channel_7_Australia "Channel 7 Australia") attracted a peak viewing audience of over 2 million in Australia.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

### Closing ceremony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Closing ceremony")]

Main article: [2018 Commonwealth Games closing ceremony](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games_closing_ceremony "2018 Commonwealth Games closing ceremony")

The closing ceremony was held at Carrara Stadium on Sunday 15 April 2018 and was produced by [Jack Morton Worldwide](/wiki/Jack_Morton_Worldwide "Jack Morton Worldwide") at a cost of A$30 million. Australian pop stars [Guy Sebastian](/wiki/Guy_Sebastian "Guy Sebastian"), [Samantha Jade](/wiki/Samantha_Jade "Samantha Jade"), [Dami Im](/wiki/Dami_Im "Dami Im"), [Ricki Lee](/wiki/Ricki-Lee_Coulter "Ricki-Lee Coulter") and [The Veronicas](/wiki/The_Veronicas "The Veronicas") were among the performers. [Prince Edward, Earl of Wessex](/wiki/Prince_Edward,_Earl_of_Wessex "Prince Edward, Earl of Wessex"), declared the Games closed and passed the Commonwealth Games flag to [Birmingham, England](/wiki/Birmingham,_England "Birmingham, England") which will host the [2022 Games](/wiki/2022_Commonwealth_Games "2022 Commonwealth Games").[[99]](#cite_note-99)

The regulations stated that from the 26 approved sports administered by Commonwealth [Governing Bodies](/wiki/Sport_governing_body "Sport governing body"), a minimum of ten core sports and maximum of seventeen sports must be included in any Commonwealth Games schedule.

The approved sports included the 10 core sports: [athletics](/wiki/Sport_of_athletics "Sport of athletics"), [badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton"), [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), [hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey"), [lawn bowls](/wiki/Lawn_bowls "Lawn bowls"), [netball](/wiki/Netball "Netball") (for women), [rugby sevens](/wiki/Rugby_sevens "Rugby sevens"), [squash](/wiki/Squash_(sport) "Squash (sport)"), [swimming](/wiki/List_of_water_sports "List of water sports") and [weightlifting](/wiki/Olympic_weightlifting "Olympic weightlifting"). Integrated [disabled competitions](/wiki/Disabled_sports "Disabled sports") were also scheduled for the Games in seven sports: swimming, athletics, cycling, table tennis, powerlifting and lawn bowls, and for the first time, triathlon. A record 38 para events were contested at these games.[[102]](#cite_note-102) On 8 March 2016, [beach volleyball](/wiki/Beach_volleyball "Beach volleyball") was announced as the 18th sport.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

The program was broadly similar to that of the [2014 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2014_Commonwealth_Games "2014 Commonwealth Games"), with the major changes being the dropping of [judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo"), the reintroduction of [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball"), the debut of women's rugby sevens and beach volleyball.[[104]](#cite_note-104)

On 7 October 2016, it was announced seven new events for women were added to the sport program, meaning there are an equal number of events for men and women. This marked the first time in history that a major [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") had equality in terms of events. In total 275 events in 18 sports are being contested.[[105]](#cite_note-105)[[106]](#cite_note-106)

### Medal table

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Medal table")]

Main article: [2018 Commonwealth Games medal table](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games_medal_table "2018 Commonwealth Games medal table")

Only the top ten most successful nations are displayed here.
The ranking in this table is consistent with the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") convention in its published medal tables. By default, the table is ordered by the number of gold medals the athletes from a nation have won (in this context, a "nation" is an entity represented by a [Commonwealth Games Association](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Association "Commonwealth Games Association")). The number of silver medals is taken into consideration next and then the number of bronze medals. If nations are still tied, equal ranking is given and they are listed alphabetically by their three-letter country code.
Australia came in first in the medal table rank with 78 gold, the second being England with 45 gold, and the third being India with 26 gold.

Two bronze medals were awarded in [boxing](/wiki/Boxing_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games "Boxing at the 2018 Commonwealth Games"). In four events of [wrestling](/wiki/Wrestling_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games "Wrestling at the 2018 Commonwealth Games"), only five nations entered the event, per Commonwealth Games regulations, only one bronze medal was available. No bronze medal was awarded in the [women's 50 metre butterfly S7](/wiki/Swimming_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_50_metre_butterfly_S7 "Swimming at the 2018 Commonwealth Games – Women's 50 metre butterfly S7"), [women's powerlifting heavyweight](/wiki/Powerlifting_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_heavyweight "Powerlifting at the 2018 Commonwealth Games – Women's heavyweight"), and [women's wrestling freestyle 50 kg](/wiki/Wrestling_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_freestyle_50_kg "Wrestling at the 2018 Commonwealth Games – Women's freestyle 50 kg"), as only four athletes competed in the event, and per Commonwealth Games regulations, the bronze medal was not available. At [women's tandem sprint B](/wiki/Cycling_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_tandem_sprint_B "Cycling at the 2018 Commonwealth Games – Women's tandem sprint B") and [women's tandem 1 km time trial B](/wiki/Cycling_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_tandem_1_km_time_trial_B "Cycling at the 2018 Commonwealth Games – Women's tandem 1 km time trial B") only one gold medal was available, as only three nations entered the event.

Additionally, two silver medals were awarded in the [men's gymnastics horizontal bar](/wiki/Gymnastics_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_horizontal_bar "Gymnastics at the 2018 Commonwealth Games – Men's horizontal bar"), [men's 100 metre freestyle](/wiki/Swimming_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_100_metre_freestyle "Swimming at the 2018 Commonwealth Games – Men's 100 metre freestyle"), and the [women's 50 metre freestyle](/wiki/Swimming_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_50_metre_freestyle "Swimming at the 2018 Commonwealth Games – Women's 50 metre freestyle")
as a result of a tie between two athletes. Therefore, the total number of bronze medals is greater than the total number of gold or silver medals.

## Broadcasting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Broadcasting")]

[NEP](/wiki/NEP_Group "NEP Group") Australia was the host broadcaster of the event. It produced [high definition](/wiki/High-definition_video "High-definition video") coverage of the event and delivered to the rights-holding broadcasters of other nations.[[107]](#cite_note-107)[[108]](#cite_note-108) In Australia, the games were broadcast live on three [Seven Network](/wiki/Seven_Network "Seven Network") channels - [7HD](/wiki/7HD "7HD"), [7TWO](/wiki/7TWO "7TWO") and [7Mate](/wiki/7mate "7mate").[[109]](#cite_note-:2-109) In the United Kingdom, [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") provided Commonwealth Games coverage of more than 200 hours across [BBC One](/wiki/BBC_One "BBC One"), [BBC Two](/wiki/BBC_Two "BBC Two"), [BBC Red Button](/wiki/BBC_Red_Button "BBC Red Button"), [BBC Sport](/wiki/BBC_Sport "BBC Sport") website, [BBC iPlayer](/wiki/BBC_iPlayer "BBC iPlayer") and [BBC radio](/wiki/BBC_Radio "BBC Radio").[[110]](#cite_note-:3-110) ESPN provided the games coverage for viewers in the USA.[[111]](#cite_note-:4-111) [Sony Pictures Networks India](/wiki/Sony_Pictures_Networks_India "Sony Pictures Networks India") broadcast the games for the viewers in India on three channels - [Sony Six](/wiki/Sony_Six "Sony Six"), [Sony Ten 2](/wiki/Sony_Ten#Sony_Ten_2 "Sony Ten") in English and [Sony Ten 3](/wiki/Sony_Ten#Sony_Ten_3 "Sony Ten") in Hindi.[[112]](#cite_note-:5-112)

[Flow](/wiki/Flow_(brand) "Flow (brand)") Sports provided games coverage in the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") countries and territories such as [Anguilla](/wiki/Anguilla "Anguilla"), [Antigua and Barbuda](/wiki/Antigua_and_Barbuda "Antigua and Barbuda"), [Aruba](/wiki/Aruba "Aruba"), [The Bahamas](/wiki/The_Bahamas "The Bahamas"), [Barbados](/wiki/Barbados "Barbados"), [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda"), [British Virgin Islands](/wiki/British_Virgin_Islands "British Virgin Islands"), [Cayman Islands](/wiki/Cayman_Islands "Cayman Islands"), [Dominica](/wiki/Dominica "Dominica"), [Dominican Republic](/wiki/Dominican_Republic "Dominican Republic"), [Grenada](/wiki/Grenada "Grenada"), [Guyana](/wiki/Guyana "Guyana"), [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica"), [Montserrat](/wiki/Montserrat "Montserrat"), [Saint Kitts and Nevis](/wiki/Saint_Kitts_and_Nevis "Saint Kitts and Nevis"), [Saint Vincent and the Grenadines](/wiki/Saint_Vincent_and_the_Grenadines "Saint Vincent and the Grenadines"), [Saint Lucia](/wiki/Saint_Lucia "Saint Lucia"), [Suriname](/wiki/Suriname "Suriname"), [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago "Trinidad and Tobago") along [Turks and Caicos](/wiki/Turks_and_Caicos "Turks and Caicos"). Commonwealth Games display at a [Woolworths supermarket](/wiki/Woolworths_Supermarkets "Woolworths Supermarkets") in [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick,_Western_Australia "Warwick, Western Australia"), [Western Australia](/wiki/Western_Australia "Western Australia")

### Motto

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Motto")]

The official motto for the 2018 Commonwealth Games was "Share the Dream". It was chosen to highlight the dreams and experience at the games that were shared by participants of the games, ranging from athletes to volunteers and the host country Australia to the world including the Commonwealth nations.[[122]](#cite_note-122)

### Emblem

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Emblem")]

The emblem was launched on 4 April 2013, which marked exactly five years until its opening ceremony. It was unveiled at the [Southport Broadwater Parklands](/wiki/Southport_Broadwater_Parklands "Southport Broadwater Parklands"). It was designed by the [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") based brand consultancy WiteKite.[[123]](#cite_note-123) The emblem of the 2018 Commonwealth Games was a silhouette of the skyline and landscape of Gold Coast, the host city of the games.[[124]](#cite_note-124) Nigel Chamier [OAM](/wiki/Order_of_Australia "Order of Australia"), former chairman of the GOLDOC, said that it was the result of months of market research.[[125]](#cite_note-125)

### Mascot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Mascot")]

Main article: [Borobi (mascot)](/wiki/Borobi_(mascot) "Borobi (mascot)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Borobi_mascot_of_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games_at_Surfers_Paradise%2C_Queensland_01.jpg/250px-Borobi_mascot_of_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games_at_Surfers_Paradise%2C_Queensland_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Borobi_mascot_of_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games_at_Surfers_Paradise,_Queensland_01.jpg)

[Borobi](/wiki/Borobi "Borobi"), the mascot of the 2018 Commonwealth Games

Borobi was named as the mascot of the 2018 Commonwealth Games in 2016. Borobi is a blue koala, with indigenous markings on its body. The term "borobi" means koala in the [Yugambeh language](/wiki/Yugambeh_language "Yugambeh language"), spoken by the indigenous [Yugambeh people](/wiki/Yugambeh_people "Yugambeh people") of the Gold Coast and surrounding areas.[[126]](#cite_note-126) The song ''Days of Gold'' composed by the Australian Duo band [Busby Marou](/wiki/Busby_Marou "Busby Marou"), which was released on 17 October 2014, was considered as the official song of the mascot Borobi.[[127]](#cite_note-127)

## Concerns and controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Concerns and controversies")]

Main article: [Concerns and controversies at the 2018 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Concerns_and_controversies_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games "Concerns and controversies at the 2018 Commonwealth Games")

Team [India](/wiki/India_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games "India at the 2018 Commonwealth Games") violated the Games No-needle policy two times. Just two days before the opening of the Games, a cleaner found needles in a bottle in the Athletes' Village.[[132]](#cite_note-132) The Games official found that the syringes belonged to an Indian doctor which he used to inject [vitamin B12](/wiki/Vitamin_B12 "Vitamin B12") to an Indian athlete and did not dispose them properly after using them and violated the Games no-needle policy. As a result, the Indian team officials received serious warnings from the Games officials.[[133]](#cite_note-133) Later two Indian athletes were suspended from the Games as needles were found in their apartment which was also against the Games policy.[[134]](#cite_note-134)

At least 13 athletes from four countries - [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games "Cameroon at the 2018 Commonwealth Games"), [Uganda](/wiki/Uganda_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games "Uganda at the 2018 Commonwealth Games"), [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games "Rwanda at the 2018 Commonwealth Games"), and [Sierra Leone](/wiki/Sierra_Leone_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games "Sierra Leone at the 2018 Commonwealth Games") - absconded during or immediately after the Games. Some missed their competitions.[[135]](#cite_note-135) A month after the games ended, officials estimated that 50 athletes had remained in Australia illegally, with another 200 staying in the country on visas.[[136]](#cite_note-136)[[137]](#cite_note-137) In October 2019, it was found from the official documents that the [Department of Home Affairs](/wiki/Department_of_Home_Affairs_(Australia) "Department of Home Affairs (Australia)") had rejected the asylum claims of 217 out of 230 athletes.[[138]](#cite_note-138) The official documents also found that 17 "unlawful non-citizens" who took part in the Commonwealth Games were still in Australia, 14 of which were from Ghana and Rwanda. A total of 13 remain unaccounted for, while four were in detention.[[139]](#cite_note-139)

The organising committee of the Games decided to bring in the athletes before the start of the closing ceremony. This caused an uproar on social media as, contrary to public expectations, none of the athletes were shown entering the stadium during the ceremony. Broadcast rights holders [Channel 7](/wiki/Seven_Network "Seven Network") complained on air about the decision and concluded that, "it hasn't really lived up to expectations". Many spectators and athletes left during the ceremony, resulting in a half-empty stadium for much of the event.[[140]](#cite_note-140) Following this, the [ABC](/wiki/ABC_News_(Australia) "ABC News (Australia)") claimed that Channel 7 was briefed on the closing ceremony schedule,[[141]](#cite_note-141) a claim which Channel 7 later refuted.[[142]](#cite_note-142)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Legacy")]

### Events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Events")]

The city hosted the 17th [Sport Accord World Sport and Business Summit](/wiki/Sport_Accord "Sport Accord") from 5 to 10 May 2019 at the [Gold Coast Convention and Exhibition Centre](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Convention_and_Exhibition_Centre "Gold Coast Convention and Exhibition Centre").[[143]](#cite_note-143)

[Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland") [Premier](/wiki/Premier_of_Queensland "Premier of Queensland") [Annastacia Palaszczuk](/wiki/Annastacia_Palaszczuk "Annastacia Palaszczuk") announced on 9 December 2019 that the state of Queensland would make an official bid for the [2032 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2032_Summer_Olympics "2032 Summer Olympics") featuring venues across [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane"), [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast,_Queensland "Gold Coast, Queensland") and [Sunshine Coast](/wiki/Sunshine_Coast,_Queensland "Sunshine Coast, Queensland").[[144]](#cite_note-144) Without rival cities, the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") (IOC) awarded the 2032 Olympics to Brisbane.[[145]](#cite_note-145)
